caveats: a: Four-digit occupation shares are not six-digit wage-weighted task shares.
a: The current level of automation is not separately measured.
rho: Large electrified-drivetrain suppliers likely adopt faster than the screened population.
e: The frozen count is margin-bridged and likely includes heterogeneous products and ownership structures.
s5: No observed six-digit transfer-rate source was available.
q: Aftermarket and low-volume specialty products can retain substantially more than high-volume build-to-print programs.
d5: EIA projections are scenario-dependent and policy-sensitive.
d5: The NAICS basket combines products with sharply different electrification exposure.
o: Electrification-driven quantity loss is captured in d5 and is not repeated here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B supply to vehicle OEMs, tier-one integrators, rebuilders, distributors, fleets, and aftermarket customers, screened for external-customer firms plausibly producing $1-10 million of normalized EBITDA.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM operations; complete-vehicle assembly; engine manufacturing outside this code; repair shops; pure distributors; software-only businesses; and firms dominated by unrelated parts.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. manufacturers of transmissions, clutches, torque converters, gears, differentials, axles, drive shafts, transfer cases, and other motor-vehicle power-train parts or tested subassemblies.

a.bridge: BLS measures broader NAICS 3363 rather than 336350: production occupations are 63.07% of 557,020 jobs, including 30.51% assemblers/fabricators, 4.26% inspectors/testers, 2.32% multiple-machine-tool operators, and 2.07% CNC operators/programmers. I translate this embodied occupation mix into a minority wage-weighted AI-exposed task share, chiefly engineering, quality records, quoting, scheduling, procurement, and machine-program support. The proxy can overstate exposure in manual small plants or understate it in engineering-heavy electrified-drivetrain suppliers.
caveats: Four-digit occupation shares are not six-digit wage-weighted task shares.
The current level of automation is not separately measured.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit occupation and wage data plus task-level time studies at independent transmission, gear, axle, and driveline plants.
a.rationale: AI can assist complex information workflows and inspection triage, but precision machining, heat treatment, assembly, material handling, metrology, endurance testing, and accountable release remain physical.

d5.bridge: EIA's AEO2026 scenarios project battery-electric vehicles reaching about 50% of light-duty sales by 2032 in most cases, while only 46% of on-road stock is electric even 28 years later; DOE says all-electric vehicles have far fewer moving parts and fluids, and identifies the EV powertrain as battery, power electronics, motor, transmission, drive shafts, and wheels. I map the rapid new-sales mix change but slow fleet turnover into a five-year decline in the current mixed transmission/power-train basket, moderated by continuing hybrid/ICE production, aftermarket demand, and EV driveline content. The sales proxy may overstate decline for EV-compatible axle/gear/shaft makers and understate it for ICE-transmission specialists.
caveats: EIA projections are scenario-dependent and policy-sensitive.
The NAICS basket combines products with sharply different electrification exposure.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level five-year production and aftermarket forecasts by ICE, hybrid, and BEV platform, mapped to candidate firms' revenue and content per vehicle.
d5.rationale: Electrification removes or simplifies many conventional transmission and power-train components, but it preserves motors, reduction gears, differentials, axles, shafts, bearings, housings, and a large legacy installed base.

caveats: The frozen count is margin-bridged and likely includes heterogeneous products and ownership structures.
e.change_evidence: Ownership, revenue, EBITDA, manufacturing activity, customer mix, and product-platform verification across the frozen 123-firm universe.
e.rationale: A substantial group of independent machining and driveline specialists should meet repeat external-supply criteria, but captive/global-tier plants, distributors, diversified groups, distressed legacy-only suppliers, and firms outside the EBITDA band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336350 ($6,578,881,922) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336350 ($44,474,585,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $553,462 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336350); class <5: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $800,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $72,398 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,785,736 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $161,431 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,229,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $291,975 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 74 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,625,527 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,412,548 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $90,474,531 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,178,898 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $689,135,446 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,297,844 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 123

caveats: Electrification-driven quantity loss is captured in d5 and is not repeated here.
o.change_evidence: Platform sourcing evidence showing OEM insourcing or fully integrated e-drive modules displacing independent component manufacturers.
o.rationale: Remaining year-five physical driveline demand still requires accountable manufacturers, process control, traceability, and testing. Some work may be vertically integrated into OEM or large e-drive modules, while software does not itself manufacture precision hardware.

caveats: Aftermarket and low-volume specialty products can retain substantially more than high-volume build-to-print programs.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, annual cost-down clauses, pricing history after process improvements, concentration, and gross-margin bridges for candidate firms.
q.rationale: OEM and tier customers have strong cost-down and rebid leverage. Qualification, tooling ownership, difficult tolerances, proprietary processes, aftermarket relationships, and program switching costs permit partial retention, but legacy capacity may compete aggressively for volume.

caveats: Large electrified-drivetrain suppliers likely adopt faster than the screened population.
rho.change_evidence: Workflow-level deployment results at lower-middle-market power-train suppliers, including validation errors, review time, capex, and integration cost.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be material in planning, engineering search, documentation, quoting, predictive maintenance, and quality analytics, but validation, proprietary data, customer approvals, legacy equipment, and change capacity constrain deployment.

caveats: No observed six-digit transfer-rate source was available.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed control transactions, owner-age data, succession status, and sale outcomes for independent U.S. driveline and transmission suppliers.
s5.rationale: Succession needs, strategic consolidation, and transition-driven divestitures can generate control sales, while electrification exposure, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and program dependence can make firms untransferable.

principal_driver: A relatively broad firm base and durable demand for precision driveline hardware support continued operator need.
principal_weakness: Electrification can structurally reduce conventional transmission content faster than AI productivity or aftermarket demand can compensate.

run_meta.title: Motor Vehicle Transmission and Power Train Parts Manufacturing

sources.supports: Broader motor-vehicle-parts employment totals 557,020; production occupations are 63.07%, assemblers/fabricators 30.51%, inspectors/testers 4.26%, multiple-machine-tool operators 2.32%, and CNC operators/programmers 2.07%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 336300

sources.supports: EIA reports battery-electric vehicles reach 50% of light-duty sales by 2032 in most cases, while reaching 46% of on-road stock takes an additional 28 years, supporting rapid sales-mix but slow fleet-turnover assumptions.
sources.title: Annual Energy Outlook 2026 Narrative

sources.supports: DOE states all-electric vehicles require less maintenance because they have fewer fluids and far fewer moving parts than conventional fuel-engine vehicles.
sources.title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Maintenance and Safety of Electric Vehicles

sources.supports: DOE describes an EV powertrain as including the battery, power-electronic system, electric motor, transmission, drive shafts, and wheels, supporting continued but changed physical driveline content.
sources.title: Vehicle Technologies Office Electrification 2023 Annual Progress Report

title: Motor Vehicle Transmission and Power Train Parts Manufacturing
